hypotheses.claim: The Currier A/B split corresponds to two different scribal hands applying the same underlying cipher to the same language but with different glyph allograph preferences, predictable via bigram transition matrix divergence between A and B corpora exceeding random variation.
evidence_for: Currier B is ~2.15x larger (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with a dominant scribe and a secondary contributor
Both A and B share top vocabulary items (daiin, ol, chedy), suggesting shared cipher not separate languages
Glyph entropy difference between sections is small (< 0.15 bits), arguing against radically different encoding schemes
evidence_against: H004/H008 at 0.88 confidence argue genuine linguistic difference
Some folios show mixed A/B features, complicating a clean two-scribe split
hypotheses.test: Compute bigram transition probability matrices P_A and P_B for Currier A and B corpora separately, then measure Jensen-Shannon divergence JS(P_A || P_B). Compare to JS divergence computed between random same-size splits of the combined corpus (bootstrap n=1000).
hypotheses.test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence of bigram matrices between A and B vs. null distribution from random splits
hypotheses.test_threshold: If JS(A,B) < 95th percentile of null distribution, scribal allograph variation explains split; if JS(A,B) > 99th percentile, genuine encoding or language difference is indicated
tests_remaining: Compute bigram transition probability matrices P_A and P_B for Currier A and B corpora separately, then measure Jensen-Shannon divergence JS(P_A || P_B). Compare to JS divergence computed between random same-size splits of the combined corpus (bootstrap n=1000).
hypotheses.reasoning: If allograph variation alone explains A/B, the solution space collapses to a single language+cipher; if not, we must model two encoding streams, doubling complexity. This test directly arbitrates between the two highest-confidence competing hypotheses.

hypotheses.claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic suffix-stripping or abbreviation convention in which a small set of word-final glyph sequences (e.g., -aiin, -dy, -ol) are optionally dropped, making many hapax legomena morphological variants of ~30% of the vocabulary rather than distinct words.
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o account for most endings; -aiin and -dy endings on top words suggest paradigmatic variation
Zipf exponent 0.895 is lower than typical natural languages (~1.0-1.1), consistent with vocabulary inflation from morphological variants being counted as separate types
If hapaxes were collapsed by stripping the top-5 word-final sequences, estimated unique vocabulary would drop to ~2,500-3,000, consistent with a medieval technical vocabulary
evidence_against: High hapax ratio could reflect homophones or null cipher rather than morphology
If the manuscript is meaningless, hapax inflation needs no structural explanation
hypotheses.test: Cluster the 8,261 unique word types by longest common prefix (LCP >= 3 glyphs). Compute the fraction of hapax legomena that share a prefix with a high-frequency word (frequency >= 10). Compare to expected fraction under random vocabulary of the same size and Zipf exponent.
hypotheses.test_metric: Fraction of hapaxes with LCP >= 3 to any word with frequency >= 10, versus bootstrap expectation
hypotheses.test_threshold: If observed fraction exceeds expected fraction by > 2 standard deviations, systematic morphological derivation is supported; otherwise hapax inflation is incidental
tests_remaining: Cluster the 8,261 unique word types by longest common prefix (LCP >= 3 glyphs). Compute the fraction of hapax legomena that share a prefix with a high-frequency word (frequency >= 10). Compare to expected fraction under random vocabulary of the same size and Zipf exponent.
hypotheses.reasoning: Confirming morphological structure would reduce effective vocabulary size by ~60%, sharply constraining the cipher and language family; denial would support a null or homophonic cipher model.

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) reflects a label-only encoding where Voynich words are one-to-one mappings to a closed set of astrological or calendrical terms (month names, zodiac signs, star names), rather than running prose, making it the most tractable section for frequency-matching decipherment.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” approximately 108 words/folio vs 84 for herbal â€” consistent with sparse labeling
Low entropy (3.7149 bits) is consistent with a constrained closed-class vocabulary
Prior belief [0.616] already partially supports this interpretation
12 folios mapping to 12 zodiac signs gives ~108 words each, plausibly 12-15 unique labels per sign repeated across nymphs or divisions
evidence_against: Label-only encoding would predict even lower type-token ratio than observed
If labels are in an unknown language or use homophones, frequency matching to known astrological term sets will fail
hypotheses.test: Compute the type-token ratio (TTR) and rank-frequency slope for zodiac section words. Compare TTR and slope to: (a) a synthetic label corpus constructed from Latin zodiac/calendar terminology repeated with nymph-count weighting, and (b) the herbal section as a prose baseline.
hypotheses.test_metric: TTR and Zipf slope for zodiac vs. synthetic label corpus vs. herbal prose corpus
hypotheses.test_threshold: If zodiac TTR falls within 1 SD of the synthetic label corpus TTR (expected ~0.25-0.40 for 1,297 tokens) and differs from herbal by > 2 SD, label-encoding hypothesis is supported
tests_remaining: Compute the type-token ratio (TTR) and rank-frequency slope for zodiac section words. Compare TTR and slope to: (a) a synthetic label corpus constructed from Latin zodiac/calendar terminology repeated with nymph-count weighting, and (b) the herbal section as a prose baseline.
hypotheses.reasoning: A confirmed label structure in zodiac would provide a constrained 12-24 word partial plaintext, enabling frequency-attack bootstrapping for the rest of the manuscript â€” the highest leverage single finding available.

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) contains unenciphered or minimally enciphered natural language prose, distinguishable from other sections by significantly higher conditional entropy H(glyph | preceding glyph) that approaches values for unenciphered medieval Latin or Italian.
evidence_for: Entropy 3.9016 bits is closest to Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95) of all sections per current beliefs
Prior belief [0.622]/[0.673] already supports this at moderate confidence
Text-only section has 7 folios with no illustrations, suggesting different compositional intent â€” possibly a key, index, or plaintext introduction
evidence_against: Small sample (2,349 words / 7 folios) limits statistical power
Entropy difference from other sections is only ~0.14 bits, within range of cipher-induced variation
hypotheses.test: Compute second-order conditional entropy H2 = H(g_i | g_{i-1}) for text-only section and compare to all other sections. Additionally compute mutual information MI(g_i; g_{i+2}) to detect skip-bigram structure. Benchmark against MI values computed from enciphered Latin (simple substitution applied to a medieval Latin corpus) and plaintext Latin.
hypotheses.test_metric: Second-order conditional glyph entropy H2 and skip-bigram mutual information MI(g_i; g_{i+2}) for text-only vs. all other sections vs. enciphered/plaintext Latin benchmarks
hypotheses.test_threshold: If text-only H2 is > 1.5 bits and within 0.1 bits of plaintext Latin H2, and significantly higher than other Voynich sections, minimal-encipherment hypothesis is supported
tests_remaining: Compute second-order conditional entropy H2 = H(g_i | g_{i-1}) for text-only section and compare to all other sections. Additionally compute mutual information MI(g_i; g_{i+2}) to detect skip-bigram structure. Benchmark against MI values computed from enciphered Latin (simple substitution applied to a medieval Latin corpus) and plaintext Latin.
hypotheses.reasoning: If confirmed, the text-only section becomes the primary decipherment target; the differential H2 signature between sections would also reveal which sections share the same encoding method, partitioning the problem.

hypotheses.claim: The dominance of word-initial glyphs o, c, q, s, d and word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o reflects a Vigenere-style polyalphabetic cipher in which cipher-alphabet assignment is positionally determined within the word (position 1, position-final), producing artificial initial/final glyph constraints that do not reflect underlying language phonotactics.
evidence_for: Strict positional constraints on glyphs (e.g., 'q' almost always followed by 'o', certain glyphs never word-final) are more consistent with cipher rules than natural phonotactics
A Vigenere cipher keyed by word-position would produce exactly this pattern: fixed alphabets for position 1 and position-N inflate specific glyphs at those slots
Word length distribution peaking at 5.03 characters is consistent with a fixed-width encoding block
evidence_against: Vigenere ciphers typically produce entropy closer to the key-alphabet entropy; observed 3.86 bits is lower than expected for a strong polyalphabetic cipher
Natural languages also show strong positional phonotactic constraints (Latin: -us, -a, -um endings), so positional bias alone does not confirm polyalphabetic cipher
Prior belief [0.05] assigns very low probability to positional constraints reflecting cipher structure
hypotheses.test: For each word-position p (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, final), compute the conditional entropy H(glyph | position=p). Under a Vigenere cipher, H(glyph | position) should be uniform across positions and equal to the single-alphabet entropy. Under natural language, H(glyph | position) should decrease at terminal positions due to morphological endings. Compare the positional entropy profile to profiles from: (a) Vigenere-enciphered medieval Latin, (b) plaintext medieval Latin, (c) a random glyph sequence with matching unigram distribution.
hypotheses.test_metric: Per-position conditional glyph entropy H(g | pos) for positions 1-5 and final, compared to Vigenere-enciphered Latin and plaintext Latin benchmarks
hypotheses.test_threshold: If H(g | pos) is approximately uniform across positions (variance < 0.05 bitsÂ²) and matches Vigenere-enciphered Latin profile within 0.1 bits per position, polyalphabetic positional cipher is supported; if terminal positions show significantly lower H, natural morphological constraints are indicated
tests_remaining: For each word-position p (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, final), compute the conditional entropy H(glyph | position=p). Under a Vigenere cipher, H(glyph | position) should be uniform across positions and equal to the single-alphabet entropy. Under natural language, H(glyph | position) should decrease at terminal positions due to morphological endings. Compare the positional entropy profile to profiles from: (a) Vigenere-enciphered medieval Latin, (b) plaintext medieval Latin, (c) a random glyph sequence with matching unigram distribution.
hypotheses.reasoning: This test directly distinguishes cipher-imposed positional constraints from language-phonotactic positional constraints, which is necessary to determine whether the glyph positional rules should be modeled as cipher structure or grammatical structure in any decipherment attempt.
